name: Niger
tagline: From the green Sahara and the caravan sultanate of Aïr to a French colony forged in violence, an independent republic, and a Sahelian state of uranium, drought and coups.
summary: Niger, a vast landlocked country straddling the Sahara and the Sahel, holds one of Africa's longest human records. During the early Holocene 'Green Sahara', lakeside peoples — the Kiffian and the later Tenerean — lived, fished and buried their dead at Gobero, in what is now the bone-dry Ténéré, while engraved and painted rock art recorded a world of cattle, giraffes and elephants long since vanished. As the desert returned, the great Sahelian trade and political networks reached into the region: the Tuareg founded the Sultanate of Aïr around the trading city of Agadez, a hub of the trans-Saharan caravan trade in salt, dates, hides and enslaved people; the Songhai Empire pushed eastward into Aïr around 1500; the Hausa states and, later, the Sultanate of Damagaram at Zinder grew rich on commerce; and the empire of Kanem-Bornu cast its influence over the east. In 1899 the brutal Voulet-Chanoine column carried French conquest across the land, and Niger was organised as a colony within French West Africa, with the desert Tuareg subdued only by the 1920s. Niger became independent on 3 August 1960 under Hamani Diori, but its modern history has been shaped by recurring Sahel droughts and famines, the uranium mines of Arlit, repeated Tuareg rebellions, and a long cycle of military coups, the most recent in July 2023.

founding.label: Independence from France
founding.detail: The Republic of Niger became a sovereign state on 3 August 1960, when French rule ended and Hamani Diori, leader of the Nigerien Progressive Party, became the country's first president. That date sits atop a very deep past. Lakeside peoples buried their dead at Gobero in the Ténéré during the 'Green Sahara' some ten thousand years ago; the Tuareg Sultanate of Aïr rose around the caravan city of Agadez in the early fifteenth century; the Songhai Empire pushed into Aïr around 1500; and the Hausa Sultanate of Damagaram at Zinder and the influence of Kanem-Bornu shaped the south and east. The colonial territory itself was assembled by France after the violent Voulet-Chanoine mission of 1899, organised as a military territory and then a colony within French West Africa, with the desert north pacified only in the 1920s. Independence in 1960 grew out of the post-war reforms that gave France's African territories autonomy within the French Community before full sovereignty.

quickFacts.label: Independence
quickFacts.value: From France on 3 August 1960, under first president Hamani Diori

quickFacts.label: Earliest record
quickFacts.value: Holocene 'Green Sahara' burials at Gobero, Ténéré, from c. 8000 BCE

quickFacts.label: World Heritage
quickFacts.value: Historic Centre of Agadez, capital of the Sultanate of Aïr, inscribed by UNESCO in 2013

quickFacts.label: Economy
quickFacts.value: Uranium mined at Arlit since the early 1970s; among the world's leading producers

quickFacts.label: Government
quickFacts.value: Republic; military rule since the coup of 26 July 2023

eras.title: The Green Sahara and the peoples of the Ténéré
eras.standfirst: When the Sahara was green and watered, lakeside communities fished, herded and buried their dead at Gobero, leaving the oldest known cemetery in the desert and a wealth of rock art recording a vanished world.
body: For thousands of years in the early and middle Holocene, the Sahara was not desert but a well-watered land of lakes, rivers and grassland — the period scholars call the 'Green Sahara', running roughly from about 7500 to 3000 BCE. In what is now the utterly arid Ténéré of northeastern Niger, this greener world supported communities of fishers, hunters and, later, herders. The most striking testimony comes from Gobero, a cluster of dunes beside a long-vanished lake, where an expedition led by the palaeontologist Paul Sereno identified the oldest known cemetery in the Sahara, in use across several millennia from around the ninth millennium BCE.

The Gobero burials record two successive populations. The earlier Kiffian people, dated to roughly the ninth and eighth millennia BCE, were tall hunter-fishers who took giant Nile perch and other fish from a deep lake. After a dry interval the site was reoccupied by the Tenerean culture, around the sixth and fifth millennia BCE, a more lightly built people who herded cattle as the lake grew shallower. Among the graves is a celebrated burial, some five to six thousand years old, of a woman lying with two children, their hands interlinked — one of the most evocative finds from the prehistoric Sahara.

Across the Aïr mountains and the Ténéré, engraved and painted rock art records the animals and herds of this wetter age — cattle, giraffes, elephants and other now-absent fauna — while the same desert preserves spectacular fossils of a far older world, including Cretaceous dinosaurs such as Nigersaurus described from the Gadoufaoua beds. From around 2500 BCE the climate dried sharply, the lakes retreated, and the Sahara hardened into the desert that divides Niger today, pushing settlement and trade southward toward the Sahel.

events.title: Burials begin at Gobero
events.summary: Beside a lake in the present-day Ténéré, the Kiffian people began burying their dead at Gobero, creating what archaeologists identify as the oldest known cemetery in the Sahara.

events.title: The Tenerean herders
events.summary: After a dry interval, the more lightly built Tenerean people reoccupied Gobero, herding cattle as the lake shallowed; their graves include the famous burial of a woman with two children, hands interlinked.

events.title: Rock art of a green Sahara
events.summary: Across the Aïr and Ténéré, engraved and painted rock art recorded cattle, giraffes and elephants, documenting a watered landscape utterly unlike the desert that exists today.

events.title: The desert returns
events.summary: As the climate dried, the lakes retreated and the Sahara hardened into desert, pushing human settlement and trade southward toward the Sahel.

figures.name: Paul Sereno
figures.blurb: American palaeontologist whose Sahara expeditions, while hunting dinosaur fossils such as Nigersaurus in the Ténéré, identified the Gobero burial ground and revealed the Kiffian and Tenerean peoples of the green Sahara.

pullquote.text: Beside a lake long since swallowed by the Ténéré lies the oldest known cemetery in the Sahara, where two peoples were buried across five millennia.

eras.title: Aïr, the caravans, and the eastern reach of Songhai
eras.standfirst: The Tuareg founded the Sultanate of Aïr around the trading city of Agadez, a crossroads of the trans-Saharan caravans in salt, dates and slaves, until the Songhai Empire pushed eastward into the mountains around 1500.
body: In the early fifteenth century the Tuareg of the Aïr massif organised themselves into the Sultanate of Aïr, choosing a sultan to mediate among rival clan confederations. Its capital, Agadez, grew at the meeting point of routes linking the Hausa lands to the south with North Africa across the desert; it became a celebrated centre of the trans-Saharan caravan trade in salt — above all from the pans of Bilma and Fachi — together with dates, hides, textiles and enslaved people. As the once-nomadic society settled, Agadez took on its enduring mudbrick form, crowned by a great mosque whose tall earthen minaret still stands; the historic centre was inscribed by UNESCO as a World Heritage Site in 2013.

Agadez sat within a wider web of Sahelian powers. To the west, the Songhai Empire of Gao reached the height of its strength under Askia Muhammad, who campaigned into Aïr around 1500 and established a Songhai presence at Agadez in order to command the northern caravan markets. The mountain sultanate thus passed under pressure from successive empires — Songhai, and later the Kebbi state of Kanta — while retaining its identity as a Tuareg polity and a vital node of desert commerce.

The salt caravans of the eastern desert, the azalai, became one of the great rhythms of Saharan life, with long camel trains carrying Bilma salt and dates southward to the markets of Hausaland in exchange for grain and goods. Alongside this commerce ran the darker trade in enslaved human beings, who were marched across the desert in conditions of great cruelty — a traffic that, like the salt and the dates, bound the region of Niger into the trans-Saharan economy for centuries.

events.title: Founding of the Sultanate of Aïr
events.summary: Tuareg clans of the Aïr massif organised the Sultanate of Aïr, choosing a sultan to mediate their rivalries; its capital Agadez became a major trans-Saharan trading and religious centre. (Some accounts place the city's rise nearer 1449.)

events.title: Songhai pushes into Aïr
events.summary: The Songhai emperor Askia Muhammad campaigned into the Aïr mountains and established a Songhai presence at Agadez to control the northern caravan markets, the empire's eastern reach into present-day Niger.

events.title: The salt caravans of Bilma
events.summary: The azalai salt caravans carried Bilma and Fachi salt and dates southward to the Hausa markets, making Agadez and the eastern oases pillars of the trans-Saharan trade alongside the traffic in enslaved people.

events.title: Agadez inscribed by UNESCO
events.summary: The Historic Centre of Agadez — the mudbrick capital of the Sultanate of Aïr, with its tall earthen mosque minaret — was inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site, recognising its role in trans-Saharan trade and Islam.

figures.name: Askia Muhammad
figures.blurb: Ruler under whom the Songhai Empire reached its greatest extent; he campaigned into the Aïr around 1500 and planted a Songhai colony at Agadez to command the trans-Saharan caravan markets of present-day Niger.

pullquote.text: At Agadez the camel routes of the desert met the markets of the Sahel — salt and dates moving south, grain and goods moving north.

eras.title: Hausa states, Damagaram and the shadow of Bornu
eras.standfirst: Across the southern savanna the Hausa states traded and warred, the empire of Kanem-Bornu cast its influence over the east, and the Sultanate of Damagaram at Zinder rose from a Bornu vassal into a powerful trading state.
body: The fertile southern belt of what is now Niger formed part of the Hausa world, a patchwork of city-states and chieftaincies — among them Gobir, Maradi and the lands later gathered under Damagaram — bound together by a shared language, Islam, and an active commerce in cloth, leather, grain and salt. These towns sat on the regional and trans-Saharan trade routes and were drawn into the politics of their larger neighbours, including the Sokoto Caliphate that arose to the south in the early nineteenth century, which pushed Hausa rivals such as Gobir and the Katsina court northward into present-day Niger.

In the east, the great empire of Kanem-Bornu, centred near Lake Chad, projected its power and Islamic culture across the region for centuries, and many of the small states of the southeast began as its tributaries. Out of this Bornu orbit grew the Sultanate of Damagaram, founded around 1731 by Muslim aristocrats and centred on the city of Zinder. Beginning as a vassal of a declining Bornu, Damagaram expanded vigorously, absorbing neighbouring statelets until it dominated a broad swathe of the southeast.

Zinder prospered because it lay astride the trade route linking Tripoli, across the desert, to the great Hausa entrepôt of Kano. By the nineteenth century Damagaram had become a substantial military and commercial power, walled and well armed, even casting its own cannon, and it would be among the more formidable obstacles that French conquest encountered at the century's end. Across these southern lands the trans-Saharan traffic in salt and in enslaved people continued to shape economy and society until the colonial era.

events.title: Founding of the Sultanate of Damagaram
events.summary: Muslim aristocrats founded the Sultanate of Damagaram around Zinder; beginning as a vassal of the declining empire of Bornu, it would expand to dominate much of the southeast of present-day Niger.

events.title: Zinder, a trans-Saharan hub
events.summary: Damagaram's capital Zinder grew rich astride the desert route linking Tripoli to Kano, becoming a walled and well-armed trading state and one of the strongest powers in the region.

events.title: Sokoto jihad reshapes the Hausa lands
events.summary: The Sokoto Caliphate's rise to the south drove Hausa rivals such as Gobir and the Katsina rulers northward into present-day Niger, recasting the politics of the southern savanna.

events.title: Bornu's enduring influence
events.summary: The empire of Kanem-Bornu near Lake Chad continued to project Islamic culture and political weight over Niger's east, where many small states had begun as its tributaries.

figures.name: Tanimoune Dan Souleymane
figures.blurb: Among the most powerful rulers of Damagaram, he expanded the sultanate's territory and military strength from Zinder, consolidating it as a leading commercial and military power of the southeast.

pullquote.text: From a vassal of fading Bornu, Damagaram grew into a walled, cannon-casting state commanding the desert road from Tripoli to Kano.

eras.title: Conquest, the Voulet-Chanoine column and colonial Niger
eras.standfirst: French conquest reached the region with the murderous Voulet-Chanoine mission of 1899; Niger was organised as a colony within French West Africa, the desert Tuareg subdued only in the 1920s, until independence in 1960.
body: France's conquest of the lands of Niger was carried out with extreme violence. In 1899 a military column commanded by Captains Paul Voulet and Julien Chanoine marched eastward to link French possessions across the Chad basin, leaving a trail of murder, rape, pillage and burned villages. On 8 May 1899 the column massacred the inhabitants of Birni-N'Konni, one of the worst atrocities of French colonial history, in the same campaign in which the Hausa queen Sarraounia of Lougou resisted them. The mission collapsed into mutiny that July: when Lieutenant-Colonel Klobb was sent to relieve Voulet and Chanoine of command, they had him killed, and the two officers were in turn shot by their own troops.

Over the following two decades France imposed control across the territory, meeting fierce resistance. Damagaram's sultan was defeated and Zinder taken, but the desert Tuareg of the north fought on; a major revolt under Kaocen besieged Agadez in 1916–1917 during the First World War. The Tuareg and other desert peoples were not fully subdued until the early 1920s, after which Niger was constituted as a colony within the federation of French West Africa, governed ultimately from Dakar. Colonial rule rested on forced labour, taxation and cash-crop demands, while investment in schooling, health and infrastructure remained meagre.

After the Second World War, French reforms gradually opened political life. Nigerien parties emerged, and in the 1958 referendum the territory voted to become an autonomous republic within the French Community. Hamani Diori, leader of the Nigerien Progressive Party, rose to head the government, and as the Community gave way to full sovereignty across French Africa, Niger moved toward independence — achieved on 3 August 1960.

events.title: The Voulet-Chanoine mission
events.summary: A French military column under Captains Voulet and Chanoine drove across the region with extreme brutality, massacring the people of Birni-N'Konni on 8 May 1899 before disintegrating into mutiny and the murder of Colonel Klobb.

events.title: The Kaocen revolt at Agadez
events.summary: During the First World War the Tuareg leader Kaocen led a major revolt against French rule, besieging the garrison at Agadez before the rising was crushed.

events.title: Niger becomes a French colony
events.summary: After the desert Tuareg were finally subdued in the early 1920s, Niger was constituted as a colony within French West Africa, administered through the federation's governor-general in Dakar.

events.title: Autonomous republic within the French Community
events.summary: In the 1958 referendum Niger voted to become a self-governing republic within the French Community, with Hamani Diori emerging at the head of its government on the road to independence.

figures.name: Sarraounia
figures.role: Hausa queen and war-leader of Lougou
figures.blurb: Animist priestess-queen of the Azna of Lougou who led armed resistance against the Voulet-Chanoine column in 1899, becoming a lasting symbol of Nigerien resistance to colonial conquest.

figures.name: Kaocen Ag Mohammed
figures.blurb: Tuareg leader who launched a major anti-colonial revolt in 1916–1917, besieging French-held Agadez, before the rising was suppressed and he was killed.

pullquote.text: The column committed murder, rape and pillage across the land — the Birni-N'Konni massacre of May 1899 stands among the worst atrocities of French colonial history.

eras.title: Independence, uranium and the long droughts
eras.standfirst: Niger gained independence under Hamani Diori in 1960, opened the great uranium mines of Arlit, and was battered by the Sahel droughts, whose famines helped topple Diori in the 1974 coup that brought Seyni Kountché to power.
body: Niger became independent on 3 August 1960, with Hamani Diori as its first president. Diori built a single-party state, kept close ties with France, and presided over the start of the country's defining industry: from 1968 French interests developed uranium deposits in the far north, and the open-pit mine at Arlit began commercial production in 1971. Uranium would become Niger's leading export and a strategic resource for France's nuclear programme, tying the desert north into the global economy even as most Nigeriens remained poor subsistence farmers and herders.

From the late 1960s the Sahel was struck by catastrophic drought. The great drought of about 1968 to 1974 devastated crops and herds across Niger, and famine in the same period and again in the 1980s killed many and left hundreds of thousands dependent on food aid. Allegations that ministers had misappropriated relief supplies, together with Diori's attempt to renegotiate uranium prices with France, fed mounting discontent. On 15 April 1974 Lieutenant-Colonel Seyni Kountché overthrew Diori in a military coup.

Kountché ruled until his death in 1987, governing through a Supreme Military Council and channelling uranium revenues into the state while suppressing political opposition. His rule coincided with both the boom of the uranium economy and the renewed Sahel droughts of the 1980s, which again brought hardship to the countryside. After his death his successor, Ali Saibou, faced growing pressure for reform that, by the end of the decade, would push Niger toward multiparty politics.

events.title: Niger gains independence
events.summary: Niger became a sovereign republic on 3 August 1960 with Hamani Diori as its first president; the United States recognised it the same day. Diori established a one-party state closely tied to France.

events.title: The Great Sahel drought
events.summary: A catastrophic drought devastated Niger's crops and herds; the famine of this period and the 1980s killed many and left hundreds of thousands dependent on food aid, deepening the crisis of the Diori regime.

events.title: Uranium mining begins at Arlit
events.summary: After deposits were developed from 1968 through the company SOMAÏR, the open-pit mine at Arlit began commercial uranium production in 1971, founding the industry that became Niger's leading export.

events.title: Kountché's coup ousts Diori
events.summary: Amid famine, corruption allegations over relief supplies, and a dispute with France over uranium prices, Lieutenant-Colonel Seyni Kountché overthrew President Diori, beginning thirteen years of military rule.

figures.name: Hamani Diori
figures.blurb: Nationalist leader and Pan-Africanist who led Niger to independence in 1960 and governed as its first president, maintaining close ties with France until he was overthrown in the 1974 coup amid drought and discontent.

figures.name: Seyni Kountché
figures.blurb: Army officer who seized power in the 1974 coup and ruled through a Supreme Military Council until his death, presiding over the uranium economy and a tightly controlled state.

pullquote.text: Uranium from Arlit tied the desert north to the world, even as the Sahel droughts left hundreds of thousands of Nigeriens dependent on food aid.

eras.title: Democracy, Tuareg rebellions and the cycle of coups
eras.standfirst: Since the early 1990s Niger has swung between fragile democracy and military rule, shaken by Tuareg rebellions in the north, jihadist insecurity across the Sahel, and a chain of coups culminating in the overthrow of President Bazoum in July 2023.
body: A national conference in 1991 opened a transition to multiparty democracy, and elections in 1993 produced Niger's first freely chosen government. But stability proved elusive. In the north, Tuareg grievances over marginalisation and the distribution of uranium wealth erupted into rebellion from 1990 to 1995, ended by a peace accord promising decentralisation; the failure to fulfil it helped spark a second Tuareg insurgency in 2007 to 2009, led by the Niger Movement for Justice (MNJ), which struck at the army and the mining region around Agadez and Arlit.

Civilian politics was repeatedly interrupted by the army. A 1996 coup brought Ibrahim Baré Maïnassara to power; after his assassination, a 1999 coup under Daouda Malam Wanké preceded a return to elected government; and in 2010 the military removed President Mamadou Tandja after he sought to extend his rule. Across these years Niger also became a front line of the wider Sahel crisis, as jihadist groups linked to al-Qaeda and the Islamic State spread violence from neighbouring Mali and the Lake Chad basin.

On 26 July 2023, members of the presidential guard detained the elected president Mohamed Bazoum, and their commander, General Abdourahamane Tchiani, proclaimed a military junta, the National Council for the Safeguard of the Homeland. It was the country's fifth coup since independence and the first since 2010. The takeover was condemned by ECOWAS and Western governments, drew Niger closer to its junta-led neighbours Mali and Burkina Faso, and saw Bazoum held in detention, leaving the country once again under military rule amid deep insecurity and economic strain.

events.title: Transition to multiparty democracy
events.summary: A 1991 national conference opened Niger's politics; elections in 1993 brought the first freely chosen government, beginning a turbulent experiment in multiparty rule.

events.title: First Tuareg rebellion
events.summary: Tuareg fighters in the north rebelled over marginalisation and the distribution of uranium wealth; the rising was ended by a 1995 peace accord promising decentralisation.

events.title: Second Tuareg rebellion
events.summary: The Niger Movement for Justice (MNJ) launched a new insurgency, attacking the army and the uranium-mining zone around Agadez and Arlit, before a settlement was reached in 2009.

events.title: Coup ousts President Tandja
events.summary: The army removed President Mamadou Tandja after he manoeuvred to extend his term, one of a series of military interventions that punctuated Niger's democratic experiment.

events.title: Coup against President Bazoum
events.summary: The presidential guard detained elected president Mohamed Bazoum and General Abdourahamane Tchiani proclaimed a military junta — Niger's fifth coup since independence — drawing condemnation from ECOWAS and the West.

figures.name: Mohamed Bazoum
figures.blurb: Elected in 2021 in Niger's first transfer of power between elected presidents, he was overthrown and detained by his own presidential guard in the coup of 26 July 2023.

figures.name: Abdourahamane Tchiani
figures.blurb: Commander of the presidential guard who led the July 2023 coup against President Bazoum and proclaimed himself head of the National Council for the Safeguard of the Homeland.

pullquote.text: The takeover of July 2023 was Niger's fifth coup since independence — and the first since 2010 — leaving an elected president detained and the country again under the army.

figures.name: Hamani Diori
figures.blurb: Nationalist and Pan-Africanist who led Niger to independence on 3 August 1960 and served as its first president until his overthrow in the 1974 coup.

figures.name: Sarraounia
figures.blurb: Hausa-Azna warrior queen who resisted the Voulet-Chanoine column in 1899 and remains a national symbol of resistance to colonial conquest.

sources.label: Stone Age Graveyard Reveals Lifestyles of a 'Green Sahara'
Lakeside Cemeteries in the Sahara: 5000 Years of Holocene Population and Environmental Change

A history of the Damagaram sultanate of Zinder, ca. 1730–1899
France Signals Willingness to Discuss Reparations for Colonial Massacres in Niger
A Guide to the History of Recognition: Niger
sources.publisher: U.S. Department of State, Office of the Historian

sources.label: Niger: Two Years On, Ex-President Still Arbitrarily Detained
